target: WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json — two targeted paragraph-scope patches correcting misrepresentation of Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 and the physically-backwards mechanism description. PATCH A replaces the paragraph-1 opening block (opening sentence + mechanism sentence + wrong citation attribution + parenthetical about standard physics predicting both direction and magnitude). PATCH B replaces the full V51.1 Update paragraph. The ~22 Hz baseline argument (middle/end of paragraph 1) is preserved verbatim, as is the Section 2.1 cross-reference. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are unchanged by this EXP (related c3 holes H4/H5/H6/H7/H8 remain tracked separately).
source_issue: ISS-1415 (critical H1), ISS-1416 (major H3) — both target the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 citation direction and the physically-backwards D-region mechanism description
patch_instructions: Two edits to WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json. Apply PATCH A first (paragraph 1 opening through the parenthetical ending with "measured D-region conductivity changes." and its closing bracket), then PATCH B (full V51.1 Update paragraph). The ~22 Hz baseline argument in paragraph 1 and the Section 2.1 cross-reference remain unchanged. After integration, verify: (a) both existing DOIs still resolve (10.1029/1999JA900056 Schlegel & Füllekrug, 10.1007/978-3-662-03137-4_11 Nickolaenko & Hayakawa), (b) the new Pazos et al. 2019 DOI link resolves (10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 — not previously present in WIN-061), (c) the #part2 onclick showTab(selftest) Section 2.1 cross-reference still resolves. TLDRs are NOT modified by this EXP — tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict currently frame the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot without making strong directional claims about storm-time SR behaviour, so they remain accurate. detail_verdict_text is also unchanged (88 chars, already frames the storm-SR claim as "ionospheric physics" without invoking a direction — arguably vulnerable but out-of-scope for this EXP; see unaddressed_holes_note for the carry-forward).

patch_anchors.before: Schumann resonance suppression during G3+ geomagnetic storms is well-documented in the literature. The mechanism: storms inject energetic particles into the ionosphere, increasing electron density and conductivity, which alters the Earth-ionosphere cavity's Q-factor and resonant frequency. This is well-documented storm-suppression physics (Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999, JGR 104, 10111; Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002) — the dome model has no equivalent mechanism — and retroactively claiming the aetheric medium is "perturbed" during storms adds no predictive content, since no formula in the dome model predicts the observed ~0.3 Hz frequency shift or ~30% amplitude reduction during G3 events (standard ionospheric physics predicts both from first principles via measured D-region conductivity changes).
patch_anchors.after: Schumann resonance response to G3+ geomagnetic storms is well-documented in the literature, but the direction of that response — enhancement vs suppression — is not uniform across events. Storms inject energetic particles into the ionosphere, altering D-region electron density and conductivity and thereby the Earth-ionosphere cavity’s Q-factor and resonant frequency; whether the net effect on first-mode amplitude is enhancement or suppression depends on storm energetics, proton-flux hardness, local time, and Schumann mode. Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 (JGR 104:10111–10118) measured damping decrease (Q increase) and frequency increase during solar proton events at Arrival Heights — i.e., cavity-Q enhancement, not suppression. Pazos et al. 2019 (JASTP 193:105091) reported statistically significant amplitude increases at Coeneo during moderate-to-severe storms. Suppression is reported elsewhere depending on storm phase, station latitude, and mode — see Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 for the mechanism menu. The dome model has no equation that selects a direction for a given storm and no formula that derives the ~0.3 Hz frequency shift or any specific amplitude change from H(r), H_0, or λ_g, so retroactively claiming the aetheric medium is "perturbed" during storms adds no testable content over a coin-flip across the documented literature.
patch_anchors.rationale: c3 H1 (ISS-1415 critical) and partial c3 H3 (ISS-1416 major). The current paragraph 1 opens by asserting "Schumann resonance suppression ... is well-documented" and cites Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 as support for "storm-suppression physics." Both claims misrepresent the cited paper: S&F measured damping DECREASE (Q INCREASE) and frequency INCREASE during SPEs — i.e., cavity-Q enhancement, not suppression. Pazos et al. 2019 likewise reports amplitude increases. The replacement acknowledges the literature is mixed-direction, cites both S&F (correct direction — enhancement) and Pazos (enhancement) verbatim, and keeps Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 as a general mechanism reference. The dome model still has no equation to select direction or derive magnitude — that criticism is preserved and strengthened by noting it is now not even "standard physics predicts both from first principles" as the previous text claimed. The final clause about "retroactively claiming ... adds no testable content over a coin-flip across the documented literature" is a sharper version of the original non-falsifiability point.

patch_anchors.before: V51.1 Update (2026-04-08): The dome acknowledged this original prediction was falsified — when a G3+ storm was observed, the measured Schumann response was ~35% suppression, contradicting the dome’s predicted elevation. In V51.1, the claim was replaced with a prospective prediction (PRED-SR-SUPPRESS) now claiming G3+ storms cause Schumann suppression. However, suppression during G3+ storms is exactly what the established ionospheric mechanism predicts: storm-injected particles increase D-region conductivity, lowering the cavity Q-factor and reducing amplitude. This is documented in Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 — the same reference cited above — and in Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002. PRED-SR-SUPPRESS is not a new dome-physics prediction; it is a post-hoc retrodiction adopting the known ionospheric mechanism after the original (elevation) prediction failed. Credit is due for the honest acknowledgment of falsification; the problem is that the replacement claim is also borrowed from standard physics, not derived from dome geometry.
patch_anchors.after: V51.1 Update: The dome acknowledged the original (elevation) prediction was falsified — when a G3+ storm was observed, the measured Schumann response was reported as ~35% suppression, contradicting the dome’s predicted elevation. In V51.1 the claim was replaced with PRED-SR-SUPPRESS: G3+ storms cause ≥30% Schumann amplitude suppression within 6 hours. Credit is due for the explicit falsification acknowledgement. The replacement cannot, however, be defended as "standard ionospheric physics predicts suppression" — as paragraph 1 documents, the storm–SR literature is split on direction, and the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 paper most often cited for storm-time SR perturbation actually reports cavity-Q enhancement (damping decrease, frequency increase) during SPEs, not suppression. The dome supplies no equation from H(r), H_0, or λ_g that would select suppression over enhancement for a G3 event, no magnitude formula deriving the ~30% threshold, and no storm-specific mechanism distinct from the standard D-region-conductivity story (which itself does not uniformly predict suppression). PRED-SR-SUPPRESS is therefore not a borrowing of established physics; it is a direction-and-magnitude assertion that happens to match a single observation, with no a-priori constraint that would have ruled out the opposite direction had the data come in differently.
patch_anchors.rationale: c3 H1 (ISS-1415) and c3 H3 (ISS-1416) continued. The V51.1 Update paragraph separately misrepresented S&F 1999 as supporting "suppression is exactly what the established ionospheric mechanism predicts" and described a mechanism (higher D-region conductivity -> lower Q -> reduced amplitude) that is the OPPOSITE direction from what S&F actually measured (lower damping -> higher Q -> enhanced amplitude). Both errors are corrected: the replacement drops the "standard physics predicts suppression" framing, explicitly cross-references paragraph 1 mixed-direction discussion, notes S&F actually reports enhancement, and reframes PRED-SR-SUPPRESS as "direction-and-magnitude assertion matching a single observation" rather than "post-hoc retrodiction adopting known ionospheric mechanism." The "Credit is due for the explicit falsification acknowledgement" kernel-of-truth is preserved. The date stamp "(2026-04-08)" is dropped in favour of "V51.1" — the poller detection date was a minor attribution error flagged in c3 H6 (moderate), so dropping it is free. A full "(detected 2026-04-08; PRED-SR-SUPPRESS registered 2026-03-23)" attribution is NOT added here because that maps into ISS-1418 territory (H5 timestamp-forward argument) which is a separate open issue not assigned to the analyst in this run. Intentionally kept narrow.

holes_addressed.description: Two occurrences in current text misrepresent Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999: (i) paragraph 1 calls it support for "storm-suppression physics"; (ii) paragraph 2 cites it as evidence that "suppression is exactly what the established ionospheric mechanism predicts." The paper actually reports damping decrease (Q increase) and frequency increase during SPEs — amplitude enhancement, not suppression. Pazos et al. 2019 likewise reports enhancement. A dome defender clicking the S&F link and reading the abstract would find our review misrepresenting its own cited evidence — the most exploitable credibility weakness in WIN-061 as written.
holes_addressed.fix: Both paragraphs reframed. Paragraph 1 opens with "well-documented, but the direction of that response is not uniform across events" and cites S&F and Pazos 2019 in their correct (enhancement) direction plus Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 for the mechanism menu. Paragraph 2 drops the "standard physics predicts suppression" claim entirely, explicitly notes that the most-often-cited paper (S&F) reports the opposite direction, and reframes PRED-SR-SUPPRESS as a direction-and-magnitude assertion with no a-priori constraint, not a borrowing of established physics.

holes_addressed.description: Paragraph 2 describes the mechanism as "storm-injected particles increase D-region conductivity, lowering the cavity Q-factor and reducing amplitude." This is backwards from what the cited S&F 1999 paper measured: S&F measured decreased damping (which is increased Q, since Q is inverse of damping) and increased frequency. Decreased damping means less energy loss, corresponding to HIGHER amplitude, not lower. The mechanism description and the cited evidence are internally inconsistent.
holes_addressed.fix: The wrong-direction mechanism sentence is removed from paragraph 2 entirely. The replacement instead notes that the standard D-region-conductivity story itself does not uniformly predict suppression — cavity-Q enhancement is what S&F actually measured during SPEs. The dome supplies no storm-specific mechanism distinct from standard physics that would select a direction.

new_evidence_added: Pazos et al. 2019 (JASTP 193:105091, DOI 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091) — reports statistically significant Schumann amplitude INCREASES at Coeneo, Mexico during moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms. Previously not cited in WIN-061; adds a second literature data point for storm-time enhancement and strengthens the mixed-direction framing.
Explicit framing of S&F 1999 measurements: damping decrease (Q increase) and frequency increase — previously the text described S&F in terms opposite to its actual findings.

The non-falsifiability framing is sharpened: the dome does not just borrow standard physics (which was the prior claim); it picks a direction and magnitude with no a-priori constraint, such that the opposite direction would have been equally "confirmed" had the data come in differently. This is a more durable criticism than "adopts known ionospheric mechanism."

anticipated_objections.objection: By acknowledging the literature is mixed and the dome could have picked either direction, the review concedes the dome got something right (it picked the direction that matched the observed event).
anticipated_objections.response: The kernel-of-truth section explicitly credits this — the dome DID correctly identify that SR responds to storms, and the March 2026 event DID show suppression. The criticism is not that the direction is wrong; it is that the direction was chosen post-hoc from observation with no a-priori dome-derived constraint. Under the dome's own "zero fitted parameters" rhetoric (§6.1), a direction-selection this consequential should be derivable from H(r), H_0, or λ_g — it is not. A prediction that matches a single observation but could have matched the opposite observation with equal "confirmation" is not a prediction; it is a post-hoc label.

anticipated_objections.objection: Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 studied solar proton events (SPEs), not geomagnetic storms per se. A defender could argue the direction is different for the two kinds of events, so citing S&F against storm-time suppression is itself a category error.
anticipated_objections.response: Fair point that the replacement text partially addresses by distinguishing "solar proton events" (S&F) from "moderate-to-severe storms" (Pazos et al. 2019). Both studies report enhancement directions; the distinction between SPE and storm is itself a nuance the dome model does not engage, since no dome equation predicts a different response for the two regimes. The "mechanism menu" framing (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002) captures that storm-type, storm-phase, latitude, and mode all modulate the response — which reinforces that the dome's single-direction assertion has no a-priori selection criterion.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender could note that Pazos et al. 2019 used a single mid-latitude station (Coeneo, Mexico) and that single-station enhancement findings are not representative of global-average behavior during G3 storms.
anticipated_objections.response: Accurate caveat; the replacement does not claim Pazos generalizes globally. The point is only that the published literature documents BOTH enhancement and suppression under different conditions, which is enough to refute "suppression is exactly what the established ionospheric mechanism predicts." One peer-reviewed enhancement paper at Coeneo combined with enhancement from S&F at Arrival Heights (SPE conditions) is sufficient to establish the direction is not uniform in the established literature.

kernel_of_truth: Storm-time Schumann resonance perturbations are real, well-studied, and observable. The dome model correctly identifies the phenomenon class — the SR spectrum does respond to geomagnetic disturbances. The V51.1 falsification acknowledgement of W027 (elevation -> suppression swap) also demonstrates genuine epistemic discipline, and the review credits this explicitly (both in the "Credit is due for the explicit falsification acknowledgement" clause and in the unchanged kernel-of-truth framing that follows). Why the replacement claim still fails: the dome provides no equation that would have selected suppression over enhancement a priori; the standard-physics defence is backwards (the most-cited paper reports enhancement); and the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot (preserved verbatim in paragraph 1, middle/end) remains an independent Self-Contradicted basis. The phenomenon being real does not validate a specific directional prediction made after the event and supported by a miscited paper.
cross_references: WIN-038 (Schumann stability 7.83 ± 0.3 Hz) — Storm-time frequency shifts documented by S&F 1999 are ~0.3–1.0 Hz, EXCEEDING the WIN-038 stability window. WIN-038 and WIN-061 cannot both be "confirmed" because they describe mutually incompatible behavior during the same events. This EXP does not surface this tension explicitly (kept narrow to ISS-1415/1416 scope) — flagging as an available follow-up for the decider.

WIN-034 (copper firmament) — If the firmament is copper, its conductivity (~6×10^7 S/m) is invariant under particle precipitation; a copper dome should not show storm-time boundary-conductivity changes. Cross-WIN tension with WIN-061 unchanged by this EXP but available for future expansion.
Section 2.1 (#part2 onclick showTab(selftest)) — cross-reference preserved verbatim at the end of paragraph 1. No change.

PRED-SR-SUPPRESS (data/predictions.json, registered 2026-03-23) — the timing-forward nature (1–3 days after the March 20–22 G3 storm) remains an OPEN issue (ISS-1418, H5), not addressed in this EXP. A follow-up EXP is recommended.

monitor/analyst/expansions/PRED-assessment-PRED-SR-SUPPRESS.json — the prediction assessment already contains the timing-forward analysis the V51.1 Update paragraph does not yet reference. A later EXP should link WIN-061 V51.1 paragraph to this assessment.
unaddressed_holes_note: EXP-227 scope is strictly ISS-1415 (critical H1) + ISS-1416 (major H3) — the S&F misrepresentation and the physically-backwards mechanism. The c3 review flagged additional OPEN issues that are NOT in this EXP and are NOT assigned-analyst: (a) ISS-1417 (major H4, internal contradiction between the ~35% suppression narrative and Unscored-dashboard claim) — the replacement paragraph 2 no longer endorses the ~35% figure uncritically (it now reads "reported as ~35% suppression") which marginally softens the tension, but the full internal-contradiction attack is not added here; (b) ISS-1418 (major H5, PRED-SR-SUPPRESS timestamp-forward: registered 2026-03-23, 1-3 days after the March 20-22 G3 storm) — the V51.1 paragraph rewrite removes the "(2026-04-08)" attribution label but does not add the timestamp-forward argument; (c) c3 H6 (moderate, update-date attribution) is PARTIALLY addressed since "(2026-04-08)" is dropped, but no explicit registration-date language replaces it; (d) c3 H7 (moderate, H_eff averaging method disclosure) — not addressed, remains for a separate EXP; (e) c3 H8 (moderate, detail_verdict_text too short) — not addressed; detail_verdict_text is unchanged. Also unused: cross-WIN tensions with WIN-038 (stability window) and WIN-034 (copper firmament conductivity). Recommend decider queue follow-up EXPs for ISS-1417, ISS-1418, and H7/H8 in a subsequent curmudgeon cycle.
notes_for_decider: Two atomic paragraph-level replacements in WIN-061 detail_evidence. Both before-strings verified unique on 2026-04-22 (A: 951 chars, B: 1129 chars). Existing DOI links in PATCH B are preserved (Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 at doi.org/10.1029/1999JA900056). PATCH A adds ONE new outbound link: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091 (Pazos et al. 2019 JASTP). No anchor changes to #part2 or showTab calls. Character delta: PATCH A +668, PATCH B +319, total +987 chars. Projected new detail_evidence length: 3938 (current: 2951). TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are intentionally unchanged. IMPORTANT: PATCH B contains a curly apostrophe (U+2019) in "dome’s" to match existing paragraph-2 style — do NOT auto-convert to ASCII. IMPORTANT: PATCH A uses both em-dash (—) and en-dash (–, e.g., "104:10111–10118"); preserve UTF-8 throughout. After integration: (a) run tests; (b) verify the three DOI links and the #part2 cross-ref all resolve; (c) consider queueing follow-up EXP for ISS-1417, ISS-1418, and a detail_verdict_text expansion (c3 H8).
deeper_analysis:
The critical hole (ISS-1415) is genuinely the most exploitable weakness in WIN-061 as previously written. A dome defender who follows the Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 citation would find our text using the paper to support the direction opposite to what it measured. The credibility damage from that discovery is worse than the credibility damage from not citing S&F at all, because it signals we did not actually read our sources. Fixing it must be the top priority regardless of whether the V51.1 replacement weakens other parts of the paragraph.

The major hole (ISS-1416) is the mechanism-direction error in paragraph 2, which is the same error as ISS-1415 applied to the D-region conductivity description. Fixing both together is more efficient than fixing them separately, since the replacement V51.1 paragraph touches both the citation and the mechanism in the same sentences.

The rewrite deliberately does NOT add the timing-forward argument (ISS-1418, H5) or the Unscored-vs-observed internal contradiction (ISS-1417, H4), even though the curmudgeon c3 deeper_analysis flagged these as stronger primary criticisms than "standard physics." Reason: those issues are still status:open (not assigned-analyst) and the decider may have planned them for separate EXPs or for self-apply. Scope discipline matters — an analyst EXP that expands beyond its assigned issues risks stepping on decider plans and making integration harder. The replacement paragraph IS structured to accommodate later additions (the "direction-and-magnitude assertion ... with no a-priori constraint" framing flows naturally into a timing-forward attack) but does not itself add those.

On the other side of the argument (user preference, applied): (1) A defender could argue that calling PRED-SR-SUPPRESS "a direction-and-magnitude assertion that happens to match a single observation" is harsher than "a post-hoc retrodiction adopting the known ionospheric mechanism," and the new framing loses the charitable "adopts known physics" acknowledgement. Counter: the charitable acknowledgement was factually wrong (S&F does not support suppression as known physics), so preserving it would preserve a misrepresentation. The "Credit is due for the explicit falsification acknowledgement" clause preserves the honest-falsification-acknowledgement credit, which is the actual kernel of truth.

(2) A defender could argue the mixed-direction framing is itself one-sided — there are probably studies that DO find storm-time suppression consistently, and citing only S&F and Pazos (both enhancement) stacks the deck. Counter: the phrase "Suppression is reported elsewhere depending on storm phase, station latitude, and mode" explicitly acknowledges that suppression is in the literature and invokes Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002 as the mechanism-menu authority. Citing two specific enhancement papers is necessary to refute "standard physics predicts suppression"; the broader literature is characterized fairly as mixed-direction.

(3) A defender could argue the "coin-flip across the documented literature" phrasing is sneering and undermines professional tone. Counter: it is pointed but defensible — the dome provides no direction-selection criterion, so a direction chosen from observation alone has the same epistemic status as a coin flip over the two documented directions. If the decider prefers a softer rendering, "adds no testable content beyond naming whichever direction the most recent observation happened to show" is a direct substitute that preserves the logic.

(4) Dropping the "2026-04-08" date and the "post-hoc retrodiction adopting the known ionospheric mechanism" clause: "2026-04-08" was a poller detection date mis-attributed as the dome update date (c3 H6), so dropping it is itself a correctness improvement. The "known ionospheric mechanism" clause was the primary vehicle for ISS-1415/1416, so it must go — but the underlying argument (the dome does not add new physics) is preserved in the "no storm-specific mechanism distinct from the standard D-region-conductivity story" clause.
tldr_change: None. tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict of WIN-061 currently frame the argument around the ~22 Hz baseline overshoot — which is unchanged by this EXP — without asserting a direction for storm-time SR response. tldr_evidence does state "storms inject particles ... damping the cavity resonance" which is directionally loaded, but per analyst.md TLDR review standard ("Fix factual errors. Do not split hairs on nuance — the expanded detail handles that"), this borderline framing is below the revision threshold and the expanded detail in the replacement paragraph 1 now handles the mixed-direction nuance correctly. If the decider disagrees, a one-sentence tldr_evidence tweak is available: replace "increasing electron density and damping the cavity resonance" with "perturbing the cavity resonance (direction of amplitude change depends on storm conditions)." Not recommended as a blocker.
